results.title: Mismatch between yield amount deposited in shares calculation and getAccountYieldBalance()
results.reasoning: The report does not discuss the shares-vs-assets unit mismatch in YieldStakingBase::stake() for Etherfi/Lido deposits or inconsistencies with getAccountYieldBalance(). None of the listed issues reference using minted shares as assets on deposit or the per-NFT yield misaccounting across multiple NFTs in the same YieldAccount. The cited items involving yield (e.g., botAdmin wrong account, allowance pull, caps using wrong index) are different code paths/root causes and fixes. Therefore, fixing the reported issues would not address the H-01 shares/assets mismatch vulnerability.

results.title: isolateRepay() lack of check onBehalf == nftOwner
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report explicitly identifies the same issue: isolateRepay allows an arbitrary onBehalf that is not enforced to match the NFT loan owner, causing userScaledIsolateBorrow to be decremented for the wrong account and leading to underflow/DoS during later repayments or liquidation. See items 26 and 49 in the audit content, which reference ValidateLogic.validateIsolateRepayBasic and executeIsolateRepay using params.onBehalf. The proposed fix (require onBehalf == token owner or decrement the owner’s balance) matches the vulnerability description. Fixing as in the report would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate() missing clear lockerAddr
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit content explicitly reports the same issue: during isolate liquidation the lockerAddr is not cleared, leaving a stale lock that later traps NFTs upon re-deposit and blocks withdrawal. See findings #32 and #48 (“Isolate Liquidation Fails to Clear Locker Address…” / “Isolate liquidation leaves stale locker flag…”), which point to VaultLogic’s isolate liquidation ERC721 supply-update functions and propose clearing tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0). This matches the described function (erc721DecreaseIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate), code path, mechanism, impact, and fix.

results.title: Revert due to underflow error, leading to lock of the liquidated NFT
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly reports the same bug (items #25 and #39): in executeIsolateLiquidate the code subtracts totalBorrowAmount from assetData.totalBidAmout via erc20TransferOutBidAmountToLiqudity, which can exceed the escrowed bid amounts and underflow, reverting and blocking liquidation (locking the NFT). This matches the described vulnerability’s mechanism, code path, impact, and suggested fix (decrease totalBidAmout by the bid amount rather than the borrow amount). Fixing the audit’s issue would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: Users cannot unstake from YiedlETHStakingEtherfi.sol, because YieldAccount.sol is incompatible with ether.fi’s WithdrawRequestNFT.sol
results.reasoning: The described issue is that unstake/requestWithdraw in YieldEthStakingEtherfi fails because ether.fi’s WithdrawRequestNFT safeMints an ERC721 to the user’s YieldAccount, and YieldAccount does not implement onERC721Received, causing a revert. The audit content does not mention ERC721Receiver/onERC721Received or the safeMint incompatibility. Issue #1 in the audit touches protocolRequestWithdrawal but only in the context of botAdmin using the wrong yield account (msg.sender vs sd.yieldAccount), which is a different root cause and fix. Fixing the bot-admin account resolution would not address the ERC721Receiver incompatibility, and adding onERC721Received would not fix the bot-admin bug. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected in the audit.

results.title: Anyone can get the NFT collateral token after an Auction without bidding due to missing check on msg.sender
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit report explicitly identifies the same issue in IsolateLogic.executeIsolateLiquidate: the NFT collateral is transferred to params.msgSender without ensuring the caller is the auction winner (lastBidder). Entries #24 and #43 state that anyone can finalize liquidation and receive the NFT while the winner’s escrow covers the debt, and recommend binding the recipient to loanData.lastBidder or enforcing msg.sender == lastBidder. This matches the described vulnerability’s code path, root cause, and remediation; fixing the report’s issue would fix the described one.

results.title: The bot won’t be able to unstake or repay risky positions in the yield contract
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit explicitly flags the same bug: in YieldStakingBase._unstake/_repay the contract resolves the yield account via yieldAccounts[msg.sender], so when botAdmin calls forced liquidation it uses the bot’s (or zero) account instead of the borrower’s sd.yieldAccount, causing reverts and blocking liquidation. This is described in items #1 and #44, citing the same functions/lines and proposing to use the position’s yield account. Fixing as suggested in the report (use sd.yieldAccount / borrower’s account) would also fix the vulnerability described.
